body:
### Problem

I am using Windows Terminal (version:  1.25.1322.0)  / WSL (ArchLinux). Windows Terminal [supports the `kitty` protocol](https://github.com/microsoft/terminal/issues/11509). Hungarian keyboard layout uses several `AltGr + <OTHER_KEY>` combination for inserting e.g. `\`, `|` or `^`. 

The default setting for kitty protocol in Windows Terminal settings is on, however with it in neovim these modifier key inputs are sending Escape. The same thing happens when using `Ctrl+Alt+<KEY>` too.

These issues might be related:

- https://github.com/neovim/neovim/issues/11061
- https://github.com/neovim/neovim/issues/31045 

### Steps to reproduce

1. Install latest stable Windows Terminal, WSL, and neovim nightly
2. Check/Turn on Kitty keyboard protocol support
3. Set your keyboard layout to e.g. Hungarian
4. Start neovim, and try to insert `\` with `AltGr+Q` (either in insert mode or in the command line)


### Expected behavior

`\` gets inserted

### Nvim version (nvim -v)

NVIM v0.13.0-dev-652+g10d0931d44

### Vim (not Nvim) behaves the same?

Yes

### Operating system/version

Windows 11

### Terminal name/version

Windows Terminal 1.25.1322.0

### $TERM environment variable

xterm-256color

### Installation

AUR (binary)

comment_data.body:
Can you verify that the problem is not your terminal and it is really related to vim/nvim.
Does the issue also appear on nano or emacs?

Start a bash shell then test, if it does echo the correct character.
```bash
cat -v
# type altgr+char
```
You can also check with `xev` which keycode is sent, but I'm not sure if that works in WSL. This method is more precise and detailed.
```bash
xev
```

I read the kitty docs, which says that allacritty also has implemented the kitty-protocol.
With allacrity on linux with a german quertz layout, which also uses a lot of altgr-maps, I couldn't see any problems.

_It's off topic, but I would advice, that you use ansi-us or a custom remapped keyboard layout for coding in vim. I would smash my keyboard if I had to use a german layout, where every special character is mapped to altgr or have some deadkeys._

I did try with both kitty protocol turned on and off and `cat -v` did fine displaying `\` for `AltGr-Q`.

Within WSL (version 2) Wayland is used, thus I had gone with `wev` (a replacement for `xev`. This is with kitty protocol turned off within Windows Terminal settings:

```
❯ wev -f wl_keyboard
[        14:     wl_keyboard] repeat_info: rate: 40 keys/sec; delay: 400 ms
[        14:     wl_keyboard] keymap: format: 1 (xkb v1), size: 66427
[        14:     wl_keyboard] enter: serial: 48; surface: 3
                      sym: Control_L    (65507), utf8: ''
[        14:     wl_keyboard] modifiers: serial: 48; group: 0
                      depressed: 00000004: Control
                      latched: 00000000
                      locked: 00000000
[        14:     wl_keyboard] key: serial: 51; time: -1559940586; key: 37; state: 0 (released)
                      sym: Control_L    (65507), utf8: ''
[        14:     wl_keyboard] modifiers: serial: 51; group: 0
                      depressed: 00000000
                      latched: 00000000
                      locked: 00000000
[        14:     wl_keyboard] key: serial: 55; time: -1559936835; key: 108; state: 1 (pressed)
                      sym: ISO_Level3_Shift (65027), utf8: ''
[        14:     wl_keyboard] modifiers: serial: 55; group: 0
                      depressed: 00000080: Mod5
                      latched: 00000000
                      locked: 00000000
[        14:     wl_keyboard] key: serial: 56; time: -1559936231; key: 24; state: 1 (pressed)
                      sym: backslash    (92), utf8: '\'
[        14:     wl_keyboard] key: serial: 57; time: -1559936144; key: 24; state: 0 (released)
                      sym: backslash    (92), utf8: ''
[        14:     wl_keyboard] key: serial: 58; time: -1559935783; key: 108; state: 0 (released)
                      sym: ISO_Level3_Shift (65027), utf8: ''
[        14:     wl_keyboard] modifiers: serial: 58; group: 0
                      depressed: 00000000
                      latched: 00000000
                      locked: 00000000
[        14:     wl_keyboard] leave: serial: 59; surface: 3
^C
```

And this is the output with kitty protocol turned on:

```
❯ wev -f wl_keyboard
[        14:     wl_keyboard] repeat_info: rate: 40 keys/sec; delay: 400 ms
[        14:     wl_keyboard] keymap: format: 1 (xkb v1), size: 66427
[        14:     wl_keyboard] enter: serial: 70; surface: 3
[        14:     wl_keyboard] modifiers: serial: 70; group: 0
                      depressed: 00000000
                      latched: 00000000
                      locked: 00000000
[        14:     wl_keyboard] key: serial: 79; time: -1559669514; key: 108; state: 1 (pressed)
                      sym: ISO_Level3_Shift (65027), utf8: ''
[        14:     wl_keyboard] modifiers: serial: 79; group: 0
                      depressed: 00000080: Mod5
                      latched: 00000000
                      locked: 00000000
[        14:     wl_keyboard] key: serial: 80; time: -1559668679; key: 24; state: 1 (pressed)
                      sym: backslash    (92), utf8: '\'
[        14:     wl_keyboard] key: serial: 81; time: -1559668579; key: 24; state: 0 (released)
                      sym: backslash    (92), utf8: ''
[        14:     wl_keyboard] key: serial: 82; time: -1559668256; key: 108; state: 0 (released)
                      sym: ISO_Level3_Shift (65027), utf8: ''
[        14:     wl_keyboard] modifiers: serial: 82; group: 0
                      depressed: 00000000
                      latched: 00000000
                      locked: 00000000
[        14:     wl_keyboard] leave: serial: 83; surface: 3
^C
```

The problem only occurs within `neovim` (as far as I saw). 

Thanks for the suggestion regarding using a different layout, however I am using (neo)vim for documentation, writing etc. beside coding, and while learning a new layout is just effort, leaving behind <30 years of muscle memory sometimes not worth it.

hmm, ok the terminal is fine, so no need to map ctrl+alt a altgr
```json
"profiles": {
    "defaults": {
        "useCtrlAltAsAltGr": true
    }
}
```
I have no idea to be honest.
Maybe take a look at 
```vim
:help keymap
:help langmap

" You could try some of those keymaps, that is closed to hungarian.
" $INSTALL_DIR/share/nvim/runtime/keymap/
```
------
Some clanker hints below:

```bash
# switch term emulator
wsl --cd ~ -e /usr/bin/mintty
```

```jsonc
// enable experimental input, for better key forwarding
"profiles": {
    "list": [
        {
            "guid": "{YOUR-WSL-GUID}",
            "name": "WSL",
            "commandline": "wsl.exe",
            "experimental.input": true
        }
    ]
}
```

I have tried `"useCtrlAltAsAltGr": true` and `"experimental.input": true` settings but it did not help. 

Also this is a WSL/Archlinux install thus `mintty` is not an option (that comes with Cygwin as far as I know?)

Do other terminal work with your wsl setup? Test Alacritty on Windows and if it doesns't work either the problem is windows/wsl. For problems with wsl and windows terminal you should open an issue in their M$ repo. It's wasted time to debug windows keyboard stuff trough wsl abstraction layer into linux. The kitty protocol is a non standard.

Yes, I tried with both `kitty` and `alacritty` too, and with them AltGr modifier works fine - tho I would prefer to stay within Windows Terminal since it has several advantages for my workflow compared to them (e.g. embeding Powershell, DOS, remote sessions in a nice tabbed interface). 

The problem with this approach (open a ticket towards them) fails due to the fact that the very same modifier key works just fine on the CLI (e.g. with `cat -v` as you suggested, and I just wrote some `sed` regex with escaping, and it wasn't an issue at all), and with other editors (like `nano`, `mcedit`).

I am unsure where that screenshot comes from but kitty is supported in this version of Windows terminal.

Some other testing (with the help of [blessed](https://blessed.readthedocs.io/) library:

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python3

from blessed import Terminal

term = Terminal()
state = term.get_kitty_keyboard_state()
if state is not None:
    print("Kitty keyboard protocol is supported")
else:
    print("No kitty protocol support.")

term = Terminal()

print("Press and hold keys to see raw kitty keystrokes and their names (press 'q' to quit)")
with term.enable_kitty_keyboard(report_events=True, report_all_keys=True, report_alternates=True):
    print("kitty keyboard state:", term.get_kitty_keyboard_state())
    with term.raw():
        while True:
            key = term.inkey()

            kind = ("pressed" if key.pressed
                    else "repeated" if key.repeated
                    else "released")
            if key.pressed and key.value == 'q':
                break
            print(
                f"name={
                    key.key_name} value={
                    key.key_value} kind={kind}, sequence={
                    key!r}",
                end="\r\n")
```

outputs these:

```sh
# with kitty protocol turned off:
~ [🐍 v3.14.5]
❯ python teszt.py
Kitty keyboard protocol is supported
Press and hold keys to see raw kitty keystrokes and their names (press 'q' to quit)
kitty keyboard state: KittyKeyboardProtocol(value=0, flags=[])
name=None value=\ kind=pressed, sequence='\\'
# with kitty protocol turned on:

❯ python teszt.py
Kitty keyboard protocol is supported
Press and hold keys to see raw kitty keystrokes and their names (press 'q' to quit)
kitty keyboard state: KittyKeyboardProtocol(value=15, flags=[disambiguate, report_events, report_alternates, report_all_keys])
name=KEY_CTRL_LEFT_CONTROL value= kind=pressed, sequence='\x1b[57442;5u'
name=KEY_ALT_RIGHT_ALT value= kind=pressed, sequence='\x1b[57449::57443;3u'
```

And not so surprisingly the same thing happens with it than with `neovim`: after AltGr tapping `q` exits the program (in `neovim` it wants to start recording a macro as `normal q` would do) --> that is the modifier does not take effect.
Now diving into Windows Terminal issues I had just found this comment: https://github.com/microsoft/terminal/issues/19977#issuecomment-4482673459

Ok conclusion: there is no bug in neovim. Other terminals with KKP support work fine.

The merged commit for KKP support in windows terminal is a few month old... 
There are 33 open issues in microsoft/terminal/issues, when filtering for `kitty`
I didn't read them in detail, but I wouldn't be surprised if their implementation is garbage.. or better say is typical ms-ai slop software quality.

Please address this bug to microsoft terminal. 
I would suggest that you **`close this bug issue`**, because, as we verified, the bug is in the windows terminal and not in neovim (and also not wsl related).
We/I should not waste more time on this.

Hopefully your issue gets fixed.

title: AltGr + alternative keyboard layout (hungarian) + kitty protocol
